evidence_quotes.quote: exploitative gaming practices such as loot boxes
clauses that allow publishers to withdraw access to the product entirely

paragraphs.text: I appreciate the opportunity to provide feedback on the Digital Fairness Act currently under consultation. I write to respectfully highlight a gap in the proposed legislation concerning the digital purchase of video games and related media, particularly regarding issues of consumer ownership, data privacy, and service continuity. While the EU has made commendable progress in addressing exploitative gaming practices such as loot boxes, there remain areas of concern. Many video games, especially those marketed as single-player experiences, now incorporate persistent online requirements and mandatory account registrations. These often serve little technical purpose but result in forced data collection, creating privacy concerns and undermining consumer autonomy. Moreover, End User Licence Agreements (EULAs) and Terms of Service (ToS) frequently include clauses that allow publishers to withdraw access to the product entirely, regardless of whether the game is online multiplayer or single-player. This means that even single-player games, purchased in good faith, can become unplayable when servers are taken offline, despite no need for ongoing online functionality. As a consumer, I believe it is reasonable to expect enduring access to single-player content I have paid for. Therefore, I respectfully propose the following considerations for inclusion in future regulatory frameworks: ||For Single-Player Games|| - Single-player games should not require a permanent internet connection or user account for access. - Developers may withdraw official support, but the final version of a single-player game should remain in a fully playable state. - Where feasible, small-scale cooperative games should support private lobbies, allowing players to continue playing after official support ends. ||For Massively Multiplayer Online Games (MMOs)|| - Recognizing that MMOs require complex infrastructure and ongoing support, I suggest the following to strike a balance between developer rights and consumer expectations: - Game sunset policies should be clearly and explicitly disclosed, not buried in EULAs or ToS. This will allow consumers to make informed financial decisions. - A roadmap for service discontinuation should be published in advance, detailing timelines for support withdrawal. - Microtransactions should be disabled during the sunset phase to prevent consumers from paying for soon-to-be-defunct content. - Where viable and with respect to intellectual property rights, developers should enable private servers or lobbies. - If technically and financially feasible, MMOs should be preserved in a limited offline mode for archival or single-player use. While my focus is on video games, similar issues arise with digital purchases of music, films, and television content. In many cases, when rights are revoked or services shut down, consumers lose access to digital products they have paid for. This raises critical questions about the nature of digital ownership: are consumers buying content or merely renting it under uncertain terms? Transparency is essential. If digital content is effectively a license with potential revocation, this must be made clear at the point of sale. Many consumers would reconsider their purchases if they understood their access could be revoked at any time. In conclusion, I respectfully urge the Commission to consider implementing stronger consumer protections for digital purchases. The goal should be to: - Promote transparency in licensing and access rights, - Preserve long-term usability of paid digital goods, and - Empower consumers to make informed decisions with full awareness of what their purchases entail. Thank you for your attention to these matters, and for your continued efforts in shaping fair and balanced digital market regulations.
